title: Source file headers and dependency acknowledgements
description: Add a consistent copyright and license header to key source files across the codebase, and create an ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS.md that lists all third-party dependencies with their licenses. This is the final housekeeping step to make the project's licensing story complete and defensible.
acceptance_criteria: All .go files in cmd/ and internal/ have a copyright header comment: '// Copyright (c) 2025-2026 James Hartt. Licensed under the MIT License.'
Header is a single line, not a block comment — minimal and unobtrusive
ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS.md exists at repository root listing all third-party Go dependencies from go.mod with their license types
ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS.md includes a section for CourtHive with appropriate credit
No existing code functionality is changed — headers only

files_to_modify: All .go files in cmd/ and internal/ (header addition only)
approach: 1. Define the standard header: '// Copyright (c) 2025-2026 James Hartt. Licensed under the MIT License.'
2. Add the header as the first line of every .go file in cmd/ and internal/, followed by a blank line before the package declaration
a. Header explaining this lists third-party software used by jim.tennis
b. CourtHive section: tournament management platform by Charles Allen, used for Cup competition scheduling and draws
c. Go dependencies table generated from go.mod: package name, license type, brief description
d. BHPLTA acknowledgement for the data integration
4. Verify no .go file compilation is broken by the header addition
technical_notes.automation_note: The header addition can be scripted — find all .go files, prepend the header line if not already present. The ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS.md dependency list can be partially generated from 'go-licenses' or manual inspection of go.mod.

testing_requirements: All .go files compile successfully after header addition
No duplicate headers on files that already have them

context.user_story: As a developer reviewing the source code, I want to see clear copyright and licensing information in every file, and understand what third-party software the project depends on
